Content: How does the Map function process its input records?

Options.text: It processes each record sequentially and independently
It processes the set of all records in batches

It processes data using a graph structure

Content: What is the expected output format of a Map operation?

Options.text: A set of intermediate <key, value> pairs

Options.text: Replicating data to all nodes simultaneously
Running the map task on a node where the input data resides

Content: What is the recommended size for an input split in MapReduce?

Content: Where do Map tasks write their intermediate output?

On the local disk of the worker node

Content: Why is the intermediate output of Map tasks NOT written to HDFS?

It is intermediate output that is thrown away later, making HDFS replication overkill

Content: What happens during the "Shuffle" step in MapReduce?

The system randomly deletes invalid data

Content: Do Reduce tasks typically benefit from Data Locality like Map tasks?

Options.text: Yes, they always run on the exact same node as the data
Yes, they only process data from their local disk
No, they must execute on the master node
No, their input comes from the outputs of all mappers across the network

Reduce tasks must start before map tasks

It is buffered in a circular memory, partitioned, and sorted by key
It is printed to the console
It is immediately aggregated by a Reducer

Content: What is the primary function of the Reduce operation?

To transform data into graphical formats
To process a set of intermediate records in batches to summarize or aggregate them
To read data from external databases

Content: Where do Map tasks write their intermediate output?

Options.text: Directly into the final output database
On the local disk of the worker node
Into the global HDFS with high replication

Content: What does "Data Locality Optimization" mean in MapReduce?

Options.text: Replicating data to all nodes simultaneously
Running the map task on a node where the input data resides
Sending all data to a single central server

Options.text: The total number of input splits (or blocks) of the input files
The amount of available RAM on the Master node

Content: What is the recommended size for an input split in MapReduce?

A background thread starts to spill the contents to the local disk

Yes, they only process data from their local disk
No, they must fetch sorted map outputs across the network from various mappers

Options.text: Shuffle map tasks and Result tasks

Content: What happens if an RDD has been persisted (cached) from a previous job in the same SparkContext?

Options.text: Spark throws an error to prevent memory overflow.
Spark copies the RDD to a new Hadoop cluster.
The DAG scheduler saves work and does not create stages to recompute it.
The RDD is immediately deleted and re-evaluated.

Content: To take advantage of data locality, what is the preferred order of task assignment by the task scheduler?
In "YARN cluster mode", which component is specifically responsible for starting the Spark driver program?
What are the two main independent entities in a running Spark application?
What is the primary role of the Spark Driver?

Options.text: To store large blocks of distributed data
To run the "main" logic, build RDD descriptions, and coordinate tasks
To execute computations and cache data
To manage global cluster resources like YARN

Content: Which of the following is a supported Cluster Manager for Apache Spark?
When deploying a Spark application on YARN in client mode (--deploy-mode=client), where does the Spark Driver run?

Options.text: On a random worker node in the cluster
On the client machine where the command was submitted

Content: When running Spark in --deploy-mode=cluster on YARN, where does the driver program run?

Options.text: Inside a YARN Application Master on a cluster node

Content: What is the default port for the Spark Web Frontend (Spark UI) when running locally?
In the Spark architecture, what happens when you call an action like .collect() on a very large RDD?

Options.text: Data is written to HDFS in parallel
Data is sent from executors to the single driver, which may cause an OutOfMemory error
The executors compress the data and cache it locally
The cluster automatically adds more RAM to the driver

Content: How does Spark handle Python logic (PySpark) execution on the worker nodes?

Options.text: Python code is natively compiled into Java bytecode before execution
The executor JVM natively runs Python code using Jython
The executor JVM spawns a separate Python process (daemon) and pipes data to it for evaluation
Python logic is only executed on the Driver, not on the executors

Content: Which cluster manager allows for fine-grained sharing of resources across different applications and runs each Spark task as an individual task by default?
What is a key limitation of the Standalone cluster manager compared to YARN or Mesos?

Options.text: It requires Hadoop MapReduce to be installed
It uses a static allocation of resources and cannot adapt well to the varying needs of other cluster applications over time

Content: What is a Spark DataFrame conceptually similar to?

Options.text: A table in a relational database

Content: Which of the following programming languages are supported by the Spark DataFrame API?
What is the primary entry point to work with DataFrames in Spark 2.0 and later?
What does printSchema() do when called on a DataFrame?

Options.text: It deletes the schema of the DataFrame.
It prints out the column names and their associated data types.
It prints out the first 20 rows of data.
It saves the schema to a database.

Content: What is a key difference between DataFrames and Datasets in Spark?

Options.text: DataFrames use static typing (compile-time), while Datasets use dynamic typing (runtime).
DataFrames use dynamic typing (runtime), while Datasets use static typing (compile-time).
DataFrames only support Python, while Datasets only support R.
There is absolutely no difference; they are different names for the same API.

Content: In Scala, a DataFrame is essentially a type alias for which of the following?
Which core Spark component is responsible for automatically optimizing DataFrame operations and SQL queries?
Which statement is TRUE regarding DataFrame operations (transformations vs. actions)?

Options.text: Both transformations and actions are lazily evaluated.
Transformations (like select) are lazily evaluated, while actions (like show or count) are eagerly evaluated.
Transformations are eagerly evaluated, while actions are lazily evaluated.
DataFrames do not have transformations or actions.

Content: While DataFrames are displayed in a row-oriented format, how does Spark SQL prefer to organize the data internally (and in storage like Parquet) to leverage memory cache and vector instructions?
According to the syntax and analysis error spectrum, when does a DataFrame catch "analysis errors" (e.g., trying to select a column that doesn't exist)?
Why is a schema essential when working with structured data in Spark?

Options.text: It defines the structure in the form of column names and associated data types, enabling Spark to perform sophisticated optimizations.
It encrypts the data for security.
It prevents the cluster from crashing.
It automatically translates Python code to Java.

Content: When caching/persisting a DataFrame in memory, why does it typically require much less space than caching an RDD backed by the same data file?

Options.text: Because DataFrames delete half of the data automatically.
Because Spark SQL knows the schema and can organize the data in a columnar format, applying applicable compressions.
Because DataFrames only store metadata, not actual data.
Because DataFrames bypass the JVM entirely.

Content: What is a column expression in Spark?

Options.text: A native Python or Scala function that runs locally on the driver.
A way to express an operation on a column, which is then calculated by the Spark SQL engine.
An exact copy of a SQL query string.
A variable that stores the physical data of a column.

Content: Which of the following is NOT a valid way to refer to a column named 'lname' in a PySpark DataFrame named data?
Who performs the actual calculation of a column expression?

Options.text: The Java Virtual Machine (JVM) garbage collector
The Spark SQL engine (in Scala code)

Content: In Spark Scala, which of the following provides syntactic sugar to refer to a column and returns a Column object?
What is the recommended and unambiguous way to refer to a column in PySpark, especially to avoid weird failures?
Why does the expression data.where('age' < 25) fail with a TypeError in PySpark?

Options.text: Because 'age' is a string literal, and Python cannot compare a string to an integer (25) to create a column expression.
Because where() is not a valid DataFrame method.
Because 25 needs to be passed as a string ('25').
Because 'age' does not exist in the DataFrame.

Content: How should you explicitly compare a column to a string literal like 'John' to avoid ambiguity?
What does the as() or alias() function do when appended to a column expression?

Options.text: It changes the data type of the column.
It renames the resulting column to make it more human-readable.
It deletes the column from the DataFrame.
It registers the column as a temporary view.

Content: When working with Scala, which symbol is commonly used to refer to a column name to save typing (using Scala symbol literals)?
What is a key advantage of using Column expressions over native Python functions (UDFs)?

Options.text: Python functions run faster than Column expressions.
Column expressions are evaluated natively in the JVM by Spark SQL, avoiding the JVM-to-Python serialization penalty.
Column expressions allow for infinite loop calculations.
Column expressions bypass the Catalyst Optimizer entirely.

Content: Why does the call maxage.select(maxage.max(age)) fail with an AttributeError?

Options.text: Because select does not accept aggregate functions.
Because maxage does not have a max attribute; it is evaluated as a Python method call rather than a Spark column expression.
Because age must be an integer, not a string.
Because max is only available in SQL queries, not DataFrame APIs.

Content: When joining two DataFrames that have a column with the exact same name, what happens if you project it using select("dept_no")?

Options.text: Spark automatically selects the column from the left DataFrame.
Spark automatically selects the column from the right DataFrame.
Spark throws an AnalysisException stating the reference is ambiguous.
Spark renames one of the columns automatically.

Content: Which transformation is best suited to evaluate a list of conditions and return a specific value (similar to a SQL switch/case statement)?
What does the coalesce function do when provided with multiple column expressions?

Options.text: It merges multiple partitions into one.
It returns the first column value that is not null.
It concatenates all the string values of the columns together.
It calculates the mathematical average of the columns.

Content: What is the return type of aggregation functions like sum("count") or max("count") when used inside the agg() method?

Options.text: A literal Double or Long value.
An instance of the Column class.

Content: What is the primary promise and role of the Spark Catalyst?

Options.text: To manually allocate RAM for Spark executors
To act as a query optimizer that automatically optimizes data processing applications and SQL queries
To securely store data in Hadoop HDFS
To convert Python scripts into C++ programs

Content: Which DataFrame method is used to display the execution plan of a query?
What are the two main types of optimizations applied by the Catalyst optimizer to a logical plan?
Which Spark project is responsible for improving execution efficiency by leveraging memory hierarchy and whole-stage code generation?
What is the first step in the Catalyst optimization process after parsing the user's data processing logic (DataFrame or SQL)?
What does the "predicate pushdown" optimization achieve?

Options.text: It pushes all the data into the executor's memory.
It filters data at the data source level (e.g., RDBMS or Parquet) to dramatically reduce the amount of data Spark needs to read.
It sorts the data before pushing it to the join operator.
It converts row-oriented data into columnar data.

Content: What is the output of calling explain(true) or explain("extended") on a DataFrame?

Options.text: Both the logical plans (parsed, analyzed, optimized) and the physical plan
The Java bytecode generated by Tungsten
The runtime statistics of the cluster

Content: Which framework, introduced in Spark 3.0, dynamically adjusts and regenerates high-quality query execution plans using runtime statistics?
Which of the following is an example of a "Rule-based optimization" performed by Catalyst?

Options.text: Dynamically switching join strategies based on file size
Constant folding, project pruning, and predicate pushdown

Content: How does the "whole-stage code generation" feature (from Project Tungsten) improve execution performance?

Options.text: By translating the entire Spark application into Python
By disabling the garbage collector entirely
By combining multiple operators into a single Java function to minimize virtual function calls

Content: When Adaptive Query Execution (AQE) is enabled, what does it dynamically do if it detects the size of one dataset falls below a certain threshold after filtering?

Options.text: It throws an OutOfMemory error to protect the cluster.
It dynamically switches from a sort-merge join to a broadcast hash join.
It saves the intermediate data to HDFS.

Content: When inspecting a physical plan generated by Tungsten, what does an asterisk (*) appearing before an operator (e.g., * HashAggregate) indicate?

Options.text: The operator requires a network shuffle.
Whole-stage code generation is enabled for that operator.
The data for that operator is cached in memory.

Content: What is the default file format for reading and writing data in Spark SQL?
Which class is the primary interface used to read data from external storage systems into a DataFrame?

Which text-based data format is considered semi-structured, allows nested structures, and is widely used in web applications?
How do you instruct Spark to use the first row of a CSV file as the column names?
What type of storage format is Apache Parquet?
If you read a CSV file WITHOUT setting inferSchema="true" or providing a manual schema, what data type will Spark assign to all columns?
What is the default SaveMode behavior if you try to write a DataFrame to a directory that already exists?

Options.text: It silently ignores the write operation.
It appends the new data to the existing files.

Content: Why is Parquet considered a "self-describing" format?

Options.text: It automatically generates Java code for the user.
It contains the schema (column names and types) embedded directly within the file metadata.
It requires a separate text file to explain the data.
It formats data as human-readable plain text.

Content: How does Spark handle corrupted records or parsing errors when reading a JSON file by default?

Options.text: It crashes the Spark application immediately.
It sets the value for all columns in that corrupted row to null.
It deletes the corrupted file from the disk.
It skips the corrupted row and continues silently.

Content: What is the effect of using the partitionBy("year") option when writing a DataFrame to disk?

Options.text: It sorts all rows by the year inside a single file.
It creates a nested directory structure where each directory represents a specific year (e.g., year=2021).
It automatically deletes data older than a year.
It compresses the data using a year-based algorithm.

Content: Which API should you use to read data from a relational database like MySQL or PostgreSQL into Spark?
In big data workflows, why is reading a compressed file (like LZ4 or Snappy) often FASTER than reading an uncompressed plain text file?

Options.text: Compressed files skip the Spark Driver entirely.
Disks are slow and processors are fast, so spending CPU cycles to uncompress less disk data is faster.
Compressed files bypass the Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS).
Spark can only process compressed files.

Content: Since JSON files do not explicitly declare column data types, how does Spark infer the schema of a massive JSON file without expensive full-file scans?

Options.text: It assumes all fields are Strings.
It parses a subset of sample records determined by the samplingRatio option (default 1.0).
It only reads the very first line of the JSON file.
It queries the Catalyst Optimizer for the best guess.

Content: Apart from skipping unnecessary columns via projection, what specific internal optimization does Spark perform when reading Parquet files to considerably speed up execution?

Options.text: It caches the entire Parquet file into the Driver's memory before processing.
It converts all Parquet data into JSON objects first.
It performs decompression and decoding in column batches.
It disables the JVM garbage collector during the read operation.

Content: What does UDF stand for in the context of Spark SQL?
Which programming languages are officially supported for writing Spark UDFs?
What must you do with a custom function before you can use it in a Spark DataFrame or SQL query?

Options.text: Compile it into a standalone executable
Register it with Spark (e.g., using spark.udf.register)
Save it to the Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)

Content: What is the recommended best practice regarding the use of UDFs in Spark?

Options.text: Always use UDFs because they are faster than built-in functions.
Use UDFs as a last resort; always try to use Spark's built-in SQL functions first.
Write all UDFs in Python to maximize performance.
UDFs should only be used for reading files.

Content: Where does the actual execution of a UDF take place in a Spark cluster?
How does a Spark executor handle a UDF written in Scala or Java?

Options.text: It compiles the code to C++ before running.
It executes them natively within the same JVM process.
It sends the data back to the Driver to run the JVM code.
It opens a separate Java daemon for each task.

Content: Why is there a significant performance penalty when executing a standard Python UDF?

Options.text: Because Python code cannot run on Spark clusters.
Because the executor JVM must spawn a separate Python process and serialize/deserialize data back and forth for each row.
Because Python limits Spark to using only a single core.
Because Python UDFs disable the Spark Web UI.

Content: Introduced in Spark 2.3, which feature provides a much faster alternative to standard Python UDFs?
Which underlying memory format allows Pandas UDFs to efficiently transfer data between the JVM and Python without heavy serialization overhead?
How does the execution pattern of a Pandas UDF differ from a standard Python UDF?

Options.text: It is called once for each individual row.
It is called once for each partition of the DataFrame (operating on subsets at a time).
It only runs on the Master node.
It skips rows with null values automatically.

Content: According to performance benchmarks, what is the ranking from fastest to slowest when performing column transformations?

Options.text: Python UDF > Pandas UDF > Spark DataFrame ops
Spark DataFrame ops > Python UDF > Pandas UDF
Pandas UDF > Spark DataFrame ops > Python UDF
Spark DataFrame ops > Pandas UDF > Python UDF

Content: When registering a UDF to be used explicitly within a raw SQL query string (e.g., spark.sql("SELECT my_udf(col)...")), which method is used?
If you decide to avoid UDFs by converting a Spark DataFrame back to an RDD to apply custom Python logic, what is the resulting data type and consequence?

Options.text: An RDD[String], which is free of conversion costs.
An RDD[Row] of objects, which is not free because the Scala-based DataFrame representation must be converted to a Python representation.
An RDD[Column], which runs natively in the JVM.
A raw text file saved to local disk.

Content: In the broader Hadoop/Hive ecosystem (which Spark SQL supports), what is a UDAF?

Options.text: User-Defined Array Filter (operates on arrays only)
User-Defined Aggregate Function (works on multiple input rows to create a single output row)
User-Defined Asynchronous Function (runs in the background)

Content: In the Hive/Spark ecosystem, what does a UDTF (User-Defined Table-Generating Function) do?

Options.text: It creates a new database table on disk.
It takes multiple rows and aggregates them into one row.
It operates on a single row and produces multiple rows (a table) as output.
It deletes corrupted tables from the catalog.
